steps.title: Identify what the blank must accomplish
steps.content:
The sentence lists two pivotal events after a colon. The first item is the founding of the American GI Forum, and the blank must supply the year and any punctuation before moving on to the second item (Mendez v. Westminster). Two things are required: the year 1948 (Choice D — for one, the omits it, so D is out) and a clear sentence boundary before “The” to keep the two events separate.
  • Choice A — 1948, for one. The: supplies the year, sets off “for one” with a comma, and uses a period to create a boundary before the independent clause. ✓
  • Choice B — 1948. For one, the: the period after 1948 cuts “for one” loose so it appears to introduce the second event, not modify the first. ✗
  • Choice C — 1948, for one, the: uses only commas, so no sentence boundary separates the two events — the result is a run-on. ✗
  • Choice D — for one, the: drops the year 1948 entirely, losing essential information the passage requires. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the correct choice
steps.content: Only Choice A — 1948, for one. The keeps the year, properly sets off the explanatory phrase “for one,” and places a period that cleanly separates the two listed events. That matches Choice A — 1948, for one. The.
